policy_statement: Implement afforestation and reforestation in existing wooded lands, new areas, and areas at hydrogeological instability risk to increase carbon sinks.
mechanism_description: Implementing afforestation and reforestation increases forest carbon sinks by adding or restoring tree cover.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem restoration (forest regrowth) → ↑ biomass/soil carbon stocks → ↑ CO₂ removals (carbon sequestration) → ↓ net GHG emissions / ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Land management shifts to active forest restoration (e.g., enrichment planting, assisted natural regeneration, reforestation/afforestation on appropriate lands) with ongoing protection and stewardship to maintain forest cover.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased photosynthesis and net primary productivity drives carbon uptake; carbon is stored in aboveground biomass, belowground biomass, litter, deadwood, and soils, with reduced degradation improving long-term carbon residence time.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Higher and more durable terrestrial carbon sinks increase net CO₂ removals and/or reduce net land-use sector emissions relative to baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased net CO₂ sequestration and carbon stock accumulation in biomass/soils, reducing net greenhouse gas concentrations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Forest restoration → increased ecosystem carbon sequestration/storage → reduced net GHGs (CO₂) in the atmosphere.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While restoration can also improve resilience (e.g., slope stabilization, reduced hydrogeological risk), the mechanism as defined is “sink enhancement” and the dominant climate effect across policies is carbon sequestration—an ecosystem-mediated climate benefit characteristic of Nature-Based Solutions (and aligned with mitigation via natural sinks).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy’s main intent/effect were hazard reduction (e.g., landslide control) with minimal net carbon gain, it could lean Adaptation; if it were primarily timber production efficiency, it could shift toward Resource Efficiency. However, those are not inherent to the stated canonical mechanism of carbon sink enhancement.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing forest restoration specifically to enhance carbon sinks operates through ecosystem carbon uptake and storage, so Nature-Based Solutions remains the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., adaptation co-benefits, biodiversity outcomes, wildfire risk interactions, permanence/leakage safeguards) requires policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include: (a) restoration in high-disturbance areas where wildfire/pests reduce permanence, altering net sequestration; (b) land-use change causing leakage (displaced deforestation elsewhere); (c) projects framed as erosion/landslide control where adaptation outcomes dominate locally. None of these change the canonical mechanism’s dominant pathway when the policy is explicitly “sink enhancement.”
key_indicators: "afforestation"; "reforestation"; "existing wooded lands"; "new areas"; "areas subject to hydrogeological instability risk"
co_benefits: Reduced hydrogeological risk (e.g., slope stabilization/erosion control), improved watershed regulation, biodiversity habitat enhancement
instance_edge_case_notes: Could alternatively be framed as a target-setting or regulatory mandate if the wider policy text specifies binding requirements or quantified hectares; here it is stated as an implementation action without an explicit mandate/target, so treated as direct investment/programmatic delivery.
